hallucinations.description: The paper claims to use reinforcement learning (RL) with policy gradient updates as part of the training framework, but the actual code does not implement any RL component. The training loop in `train_policy_network` only uses efficiency loss and coverage loss - there is no reward computation, no policy gradient update, no baseline estimation, and no RL reward weight β. The paper describes a reward function R = Accuracy(...) - β·CacheSize(...) and a policy gradient update, but none of this exists in the code.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 3.3 states: 'The reward function is: R = Accuracy(f_θ(X', τ), y) − β · CacheSize(X')' and 'The policy gradient update is: ∇_φ J(φ) = E_{M∼π_φ}[(R − b)∇_φ log π_φ(M|X, q, τ)]'. But the code's train_policy_network method only computes: 'loss = 0.7 * efficiency_loss + 0.3 * coverage_loss' with no RL component.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims to use Meta-Learning (MAML) for cross-task generalization as part of the methodology, but the code does not implement any meta-learning. There is no task sampling, no inner-loop adaptation, and no MAML-style optimization anywhere in the codebase.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 3.3 states: 'We adopt Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) to enable rapid adaptation to new tasks: φ* = arg min_φ Σ_{τ∼p(T)} L_τ(f_θ, π_{φ'_τ})'. The code only trains on a single dataset with standard gradient descent.

hallucinations.description: The paper claims to jointly optimize the foundation model and policy network, mentioning LoRA fine-tuning of the foundation model with learning rate 1×10^-5. However, the code never fine-tunes the foundation model - it is loaded with model.eval() and used only for inference (with torch.no_grad() in get_embeddings). Only the policy network parameters are optimized.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 4.3 states: 'Learning rate (foundation model): 1 × 10^-5 (with LoRA)'. But the code shows: 'self.model.eval()' and 'with torch.no_grad(): outputs = self.model(...)' in get_embeddings, and the optimizer only covers policy network parameters: 'optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(self.policy_network.parameters(), lr=lr)'

hallucinations.description: The paper claims to use Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable sampling during training, but the code uses plain sigmoid activation for retention probabilities during training (soft mask), not Gumbel-Softmax. The code simply returns retention_probs as the soft mask.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 3.2 states: 'm_i = GumbelSoftmax([s_i, −s_i], τ)'. But the code's forward method shows: 'token_probs.append(torch.sigmoid(token_scores))' and 'retention_mask = retention_probs' when hard=False (training mode).

hallucinations.description: The paper claims the training uses 'Batch size: 16 with gradient accumulation', but the code uses batch_size=1 in the DataLoader with no gradient accumulation implemented.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 4.3 states: 'Batch size: 16 with gradient accumulation'. Code shows: 'train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=1, shuffle=True)' with no gradient accumulation logic.

hallucinations.description: The paper describes the attention-based pruning baseline as pruning tokens based on cumulative attention scores across layers, but the logs show that attention pruning always failed and fell back to static pruning. The paper reports attention-based pruning latency of 0.0426s as if it were performing attention-based computation, but it was actually doing static pruning with extra overhead from the failed attention extraction attempt.
hallucinations.evidence: Log shows repeated: 'WARNING - Attention pruning failed: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'mean', using static fallback' for all 50 test samples. Yet the paper discusses attention-based pruning as if it functioned: 'attention-based pruning doubled inference latency (0.0426s vs 0.0206s), representing a 2.07× overhead. This overhead stems from the computational cost of extracting and processing attention scores across all layers.'

hallucinations.description: The paper reports the attention-based method's latency overhead as being due to 'the computational cost of extracting and processing attention scores across all layers' and 'Aggregation across multiple layers and heads, Sorting and selection operations, Additional memory for storing attention matrices'. In reality, the attention extraction failed every time and fell back to static pruning. The 2x latency overhead is from the failed attempt and exception handling, not from actual attention computation.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 5.2 states: 'This overhead stems from the computational cost of extracting and processing attention scores across all layers.' Section 6.2 states: 'Computing attention scores requires: Full forward pass through the model, Aggregation across multiple layers and heads'. But logs show all 50 samples hit: 'Attention pruning failed: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'mean', using static fallback'

hallucinations.description: The paper describes the policy network architecture as having '4-layer Transformer encoder' but the code implements only a 2-layer Transformer encoder for the chunk encoder.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper Section 4.3 states: 'Number of layers: 4-layer Transformer encoder'. Code shows: 'self.chunk_encoder = nn.TransformerEncoder(..., num_layers=2)'

overall_assessment: The paper contains multiple significant hallucinations. The most severe are methodological: the paper claims to use RL training, MAML meta-learning, Gumbel-Softmax, LoRA fine-tuning, and joint optimization, none of which are implemented in the code. The actual training is a simple supervised loss with efficiency and coverage terms. Additionally, the attention-based baseline completely failed (falling back to static pruning for all samples), yet the paper analyzes its results as if attention computation was performed, attributing the latency overhead to attention score extraction. The experimental results for the attention baseline are therefore misleading. The paper's architecture description also doesn't match the code (4-layer vs 2-layer Transformer).
